channel.title: Comments for page "∆K=([ϑK/ ϑx], [ ϑK/ ϑy], [ ϑK/ ϑz]) Or: Episiotometrics"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "∆K=([ϑK/ ϑx], [ ϑK/ ϑy], [ ϑK/ ϑz]) Or: Episiotometrics"

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Looks like it was added erroneously by someone who isn't on the tag team. I've removed the incorrect tag, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why this tale has a scp tag ("feline")?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really get much of a reaction to it beyond, "well, that was a bunch of stuff that happened."

Unfortunately this was… kinda my reaction too.

I actually liked the theoretical characterization. But in practice there wasn't much. I think the worst part for me was when Zeke dies for no reason I could discern ("because the author wanted him to" was how I felt), and Matthew just doesn't give even a tiny shit. Why did they even have that kiss then?

I agree with Soulless; while I really like the dialogue, all three voices are identical.

Mostly the problem is that it's hard to care about any of this.

That said, I still kinda wanted to upvote it because of the number of good lines in it. This is exceptional writing despite its flaws. Leaving it neutral at the moment, though.

(EDIT: I also appreciated the gay couple <3)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I'll pretend like I remember what that was," Matthew said. "I was busy with the balrog, remember?"
[…]
"Fuckin' A, the Devourer. Couldn't have happened to nicer people."

I don't like this whole section, but I can't quite pin-point why. It feels too forced, perhaps? I liked the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hell is not fire, brimstone and suffering.

It is kittens.

+1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… no. No, I think I'm gonna no-vote.

It's well written, but as Kalinin stated above, it's not much more than "EVERYTHING HAPPENS" then kitten euthanasia. I've read much better entries in this contest, and while it is very neatly written, this simply remains far too removed from my maximum silliness quotient threshold. I did enjoy parts of it, but in general, I wasn't moved or particularly interested at all.

Also, kittens as a punchline is ridiculous, but that doesn't beat other entries. Sorry, Eskobar, not voting this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's intended as a fairly dark sort of humor. It's definitely not built to stand up to real criticism; it was more or less just a fun thrown-together grab bag of "shit went wrong" with no real explanation. And then a bunch of stuff happened. And probably not even that, because alternate universes and shenanigans. There's stuff that I make that's intended to be more like real literary art, and there's…this. It is what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sat and wrote the whole thing out in one sitting, in front of the open dialogue box, no second draft, no real editing, no critique.

I could say quite a lot about this statement. I'll stick to just saying that this could have used the drafting and editing processes that you decided against using.

"Kind of," Matthew said. "Everything broke out. A couple things at first, then some attacks from some other groups, then a war, then some more things, then some more attacks, then another war, then everything blew up. Most of the species is dead. But we're going to fix it, we think? I dunno. I think you're involved somehow."

I want to focus on this paragraph, because it's emblematic of how the whole thing turned out. There's very little sense of cohesion here. The events of the story are a grab bag of bad things that happen from a laundry list of skips. To your credit, they're ones that haven't been referenced to death, which I appreciate. But there doesn't seem to be any sort of thematic reason why this scenario involved the spiders, Eastern Samothrace (I guess the line had to be shoehorned in there), the State of Franklin, the Centralia thing, etc. etc. You could have substituted just about any Keter skip and the story wouldn't really have changed at all.

And the tone of this, I'm not sure what to make of it. You have a romantic relationship which is sort of played for laughs because of how casual it is, but then it gets treated seriously at other points, and I have no idea if I'm supposed to actually care about the relationship between these two men, or if it's another element in the wild assortment of things going on here. The same goes for the line TL333s references above, which I get a strong impression is supposed to make a serious impact, but in the context it's presented in that's completely impossible. And it cuts both ways, because this is presented as sort of a humorous tale, but I can't really find it funny because there's some very real human suffering appended to it. The signal to the reader is completely jammed here.

And this paragraph summarizes my issues with the piece. Everything gets loose. A lot of stuff happens. A bunch of wars start and the chaotic wackiness piles up, but we're going to do some things. The fogginess of the starting concept is on full display, and after I read this article, I don't really get much of a reaction to it beyond, "well, that was a bunch of stuff that happened."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're exactly right. I only have one "sarcastic but world-wise" voice, and it's basically 16-year-old me, and I used it for all three characters. That's probably the worst part of having written this as on-the-fly as I did, though I'm not sure if I would change it in editing, to be honest. It really is how I imagine this iteration of these characters, albeit for different reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
100% "fuck yes" from start to finish.

Zeke would have had tears running down his face if he had been properly hydrated.

I don't know why, but this was fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, a bit. Wasn't enough for me to downvote, but voice can be hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't sure I liked the ending. Then it occurred to me that my dinner got cold cause I refused to eat until I finished this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Matthew, zeke and Tyler all have the same voice to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Matthew snorted. "Are you seriously that inbred? You didn't here about that thing down in Samothrace?"

"You didn't hear", I assume.

Very enjoyable and funny read. I loved Year of the Many, and seeing these characters again makes me smile. If the world needs to be euthanized, well, why not kittens? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Years ago, I was sitting at work at a daycare, theoretically watching some kids, when I got an idea for a story. I sat in a chair in front of a legal pad and wrote the whole thing out longhand; it became my first Tale. I had always wanted to revisit the characters, but being the end of the world, it was hard to do. Multiple SCPs reference throughout (you should recognize most of the references), and the climax involves the terribly-written but well-conceived SCP-2559-J. I sat and wrote the whole thing out in one sitting, in front of the open dialogue box, no second draft, no real editing, no critique. It might be crap; judge it for what it is, vote as your soul tells you.

EDIT: Dunno if it counts as a sequel or not, what with alternate universe shenanigans. It definitely repeats characters, but it's definitely not part of a canon as such, and they're technically different characters. I'll accept whatever ruling the judges apply in this case. (I didn't realize that part about sequels was in the rules.)
